questions.question: Highlight a sentence or phrase from the text that supports your answer to Part A.

responses.content: She feels sick inside and shuts her eyes and clunches down her teeth.

questions.question: Part A: Which of the following abilities does the narrator mainly believe older people have that younger people don't?

responses.content: She feels the sweater is hurting her and is really itchy and she is really uncomfortable.
she then cries in front of everyone and puts her face in her desk

questions.question: Part A: What is the main conflict Rachel faces in the story?

responses.content: D. She is forced to wear a sweater that isn't hers and can't effectively stand up for herself.

questions.question: Part B: Which evidence from the text BEST shows how Rachel responds to this conflict?

responses.content: A. “That's when everything I've been holding in since this morning, since when Mrs. Price put the sweater on my desk, finally lets go, and all of a sudden I'm crying in front of everybody.”

questions.question: What claim does Sylvia make about the sweater in paragraph 9?

responses.content: because the sweater is not hers and she says its ugly and not her style so she dosent want the sweater

questions.question: How does Rachel react when Sylvia makes this claim?

responses.content: she claims the sweater is not hers and says its Racheals cause its ugly

questions.question: Highlight a sentence from paragraphs 18-19 that describes Rachel's physical reaction to wearing the sweater.

responses.content: Rachel then says that its not hers in a little voice

questions.question:
Re-read the following line from paragraph 14:

“Except when math period ends Mrs. Price says loud and in front of everybody, ‘Now, Rachel, that's enough,’ because she sees I've shoved the red sweater to the tippy-tip corner of my desk and it's hanging over the edge like a waterfall, but I don't care.”

What specific characteristic do waterfalls and the hanging sweater share that makes this comparison effective?

responses.content: She compares it to a flowing waterfall

questions.question: In paragraph 19, Rachel says she's “crying like I'm three in front of everybody.” This comparison is an example of:

responses.content: Because she says that the sweater is falling down like a waterfall on a cliff
She says that the sweater is hanging off her desk just like a waterfall hanging off a cliff

questions.question: How does the figurative language in paragraph 14 help readers understand Rachel's reaction to the sweater?

responses.content: D. The simile in the paragraph makes it clear to readers that Rachel is overreacting about the sweater.

questions.question: How does the figurative language in paragraph 19 help readers visualize Rachel's behavior?

responses.content: C. The similes about her body shaking “like when you have the hiccups” and her head hurting “like when you drink milk too fast” connect to unpleasant experiences most readers have had.

questions.question: What do Brian's expectations of rescue reveal about how he views his current setting?

responses.content: Lake

questions.question: Reread paragraphs 7-9. Why does Brian evaluate his surroundings so carefully?

responses.content: He thinks that planes can landed on water and walk on water like there not human and there super humans
Fake because he thinks planes can landed on a lake
Planes the con float and fly

questions.question: How does the setting in paragraphs 12-14 cause Brian to change from feeling hopeless to taking action?

responses.content: He does that thinking there is nothing at all to eat
He is very hungry but too hydrated
Brian thinks there is nothing to eat and has a stomach full of water

questions.question: This part of the story takes place in the wilderness. What challenge does this setting create for Brian?

responses.content: C. The setting's lack of food sources forces Brian to confront the reality of his survival needs

questions.question: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to the previous question?

responses.content: A. “And there was, he thought, absolutely nothing to eat. Nothing.”
